column1: Pre-Seljuk Period & Origins (985-1037 CE)
Early Empire: Tughril Beg's Reign (1037-1063 CE)
Imperial Zenith: Alp Arslan & Malik Shah I (1063-1092 CE)
Fragmentation & Decline (1092-1157 CE)
Legacy & Aftermath (1157-1194 CE)
(Including Religious, Architectural, and Archaeological Developments)
Key Religious & Architectural Sites of the Seljuks
1. Mosques & Madrasas
Great Mosque of Isfahan (Iran) – Expanded under Seljuks, featuring iconic dome & courtyard.
Al-Nizamiyya Madrasa (Baghdad) – Founded by Nizam al-Mulk (1065), a model for Islamic education.
Ulu Mosque (Diyarbakır, Turkey) – Seljuk-era renovations.
2. Mausoleums & Sacred Tombs
Sultan Sanjar Mausoleum (Merv, Turkmenistan) – A masterpiece of Seljuk brick architecture.
Pir-e Alamdar (Damghan, Iran) – Early Seljuk tomb tower.
Döner Kümbet (Kayseri, Turkey) – Unique Seljuk-era rotating tomb design.
3. Caravanserais & Trade Routes
Ribat-i Sharaf (Iran) – A major Seljuk caravanserai on the Silk Road.
Sultan Han (Turkey) – One of the largest Seljuk caravanserais.
4. Caves & Sacred Natural Sites
Dandanaqan (Turkmenistan) – Site of the 1040 battle; later a pilgrimage site.
Mount Ararat (Turkey) – Revered in Turkic folklore; Seljuks associated it with Noah’s Ark.
5. Customs & Spiritual Practices
Sufi Lodges (Khaniqahs) – Centers of mysticism (e.g., Ahmad Yasawi’s teachings).
Nowruz (Persian New Year) – Celebrated by Seljuks, blending Turkic & Persian traditions.
Ghazi Culture – Warrior saints venerated in frontier regions.
Legacy of the Seljuks
Architectural Influence → Later Persian, Ottoman, and Mughal styles.
Religious Impact → Strengthened Sunni Islam against Shia & Crusader threats.
Cultural Fusion → Turkic-Persian synthesis in art, language, and governance.
